value: Rust Async vs. Go Goroutines: My Side-by-Side Benchmark of 3 Real Workloads

Member-only story
Rust Async vs. Go Goroutines: My Side-by-Side Benchmark of 3 Real Workloads
I built the same high-concurrency applications in both languages — here’s what 1 million concurrent tasks revealed about performance, memory usage, and developer experience
“Rust is faster but Go is simpler” has become the conventional wisdom when comparing these two systems programming languages. But what does that actually mean when you’re building real applications that need to handle thousands of concurrent operations?
I spent three months building identical applications in both languages to answer this question definitively. The benchmarks covered three real-world scenarios: a WebSocket chat server handling 100,000 concurrent connections, a data processing pipeline with 1 million concurrent tasks, and a microservice handling high-frequency API requests.
The results challenged my assumptions about both languages. While Rust’s async runtime delivered superior raw performance, Go’s goroutines provided more predictable behavior under extreme load. Most surprisingly, the performance gap narrowed significantly when I optimized the Go implementations with production-ready techniques.
